en_body:
TRANSLATION ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
........................WISLAWA SZYMBORSKA
by
........................Hoàng-Ân......................................................................
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

From: VIEW WITH A GRAIN OF SAND,
"Vocabulary" by Wislawa Szymborska

 "La Pologue? La Pologue? Isn't it terribly cold there?"
She asked, and then sighed with relief. So many countries have
been turning up lately that the safest thing to talk about is their
climate.
 "Madame," I want to reply, "my people's poets do all their writing
in mittens. I don't mean to imply that they never remove them;they
do, indeed, if the moon is warm enough. In stanzas composes of
raucos whooping, for only such can drown the windstorms' constant
roaring, they glorify the simple lives of our walrus' herders. Our
classisists engrave their odes with inky icicles on trampled snowdrifts.
The rest, our decadents, bewail their fate with snowflakes instead of
tears. He who wishes to drown himself must have an ax at hand to
cut the ice. Oh, madame, dearest madame." That's what I mean to
say. But I've forgotten the word for 'walrus' in French. And I'm not sure
of 'icicle' and 'ax'.
 "La Pologue? La Plogue? Isn't it terribly cold there?"
 "Pas du tout," I answered icily.


Hoàng-Ân translates: the poem "Vocabulary"~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~.
from the poetry collection "A View With A Grain Of Sand"
by the great poet Wislawa Szymborska...............................................
................................................................................................................

 "La Pologue? La Pologue? Is it terribly cold there?" the lady asked,
and breathed out in comfort, having finished a proper opening greeting. These days
far too many countries have risen from their graves; therefore the safest thing
to speak about is Climate.
 "Madame," I wish to answer, "The poets of my people's country work
wearing woolen mittens. I do not mean that they never take them off;
in truth they do, if the moon is warm enough. In stanzas
of crooked, raucous whooping, because only thus can they overwhelm the roar
of the timely windstorms forever raging beyond the horizon;
they glorify the simple life of our otter-herders.
Our 'classicists' carve their sacrificial odes of praise
with ink-soaked icicles upon lost snowdrifts writhing beneath erasure. The
rest, our 'decadents,' lament their Fate
with snowflakes instead of tears. Whoever wishes to drown
must hold in his own hand an ax to break through the stony ice. Oh, Madame,
my dearest Madame."

 That is what I wish to say! But I have wholly forgotten the word for 'otter' in
French. And I cannot surely remember the words for 'icicle' and 'ax.'

 "La Pologue? La Pologue? Is it terribly cold there?"
 "Pas du tout," I answered, ice-cold.
note: The poem turns a foreigner's stereotype of Poland into an elaborate Arctic fantasy before ending with the French phrase “not at all.”
